medications.indication_rationale: The single most important intervention for this flare is replacing diarrhoeal fluid losses. Advise frequent small sips of an oral rehydration solution (or clear electrolyte fluids) rather than large volumes, which provoke cramps. This is patient-actionable and directly addresses the dehydration that is the only acute risk.
medications.dose_route_frequency: oral — small frequent sips (e.g. 5-10 mL every few minutes, building up as tolerated) over the next 24 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose amitriptyline is the note's clinician-selected agent for visceral pain modulation in post-infectious IBS, to be INITIATED by the GP at review (not started acutely in a telehealth chat). Dose is taken verbatim from the source note. The AI Doctor should frame it as a GP-review recommendation, not prescribe it directly.
medications.dose_route_frequency: amitriptyline 10 mg orally at night (as documented by the source clinician; GP to initiate at review)

medications.indication_rationale: An antispasmodic can relieve the crampy pain of a functional flare. The source clinician gave it IM 20 mg in person; the AI Doctor CANNOT administer parenteral medication. Where symptom relief is needed, an oral antispasmodic is a patient-accessible option to raise with the GP, and the parenteral route is documented as the in-person pathway only.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 20 mg intramuscularly (clinician-administered in person per source note); oral antispasmodic is the telehealth-appropriate alternative to discuss with the GP

medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician gave sublingual ondansetron 4 mg for nausea/vomiting that was preventing oral intake. An antiemetic to enable oral rehydration is reasonable, but is a prescribing decision for the reviewing GP — the AI Doctor recommends review rather than issuing it. Dose taken verbatim from the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ondansetron 4 mg sublingually (as documented by the source clinician; GP prescribing decision)

medications.indication_rationale: The patient already tried loperamide and vomited it. Antimotility agents are not the answer to a functional flare, are ineffective if they cannot be kept down, and are inappropriate if any infective component is possible. Pushing loperamide as the fix is an error of commission; priority is rehydration and review.

There is no evidence of bacterial infection (afebrile, no sick contacts, stereotyped non-infectious flare). Empirical antibiotics are not indicated, risk harm and antimicrobial resistance, and would be an error of commission.

Opioids worsen gut dysmotility, cause constipation and nausea, and are inappropriate for functional visceral pain. Antispasmodic and neuromodulator strategies are preferred; opioids are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: For the next 24 hours take only clear, room-temperature electrolyte fluids in small frequent sips; reintroduce bland low-residue food gradually as cramps settle.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Resting the hyper-reactive gut and prioritising rehydration reduces cramp-provoking stimuli and replaces fluid losses; room-temperature fluids are better tolerated than cold ones.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Monitor for the escalation triggers — inability to keep any fluids down, worsening dizziness or fainting, markedly reduced urination, blood in stool or vomit, or a hard/rigid painful abdomen.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Empowers a patient living alone to recognise deterioration and act on the safety-net promptly.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Because you live alone and are dizzy, ask a friend or neighbour to check in on you, keep your phone within reach, and move slowly when standing to avoid a fall.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces orthostatic-fall risk and unwitnessed-collapse risk in a person who is dizzy and living alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At GP review, discuss a longer-term plan for the post-infectious/mast-cell picture — trigger identification, a dietitian-guided dietary strategy (e.g. low-FODMAP trial), and neuromodulation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Post-infectious IBS is managed longitudinally in outpatient care; a structured plan reduces flare frequency and severity.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Named in the source note's targeted strategy as a mast-cell/neuro-inflammation adjunct. Evidence in functional GI disease is limited; reasonable to discuss with the GP as an adjunct, not a core treatment.
Named in the source note's strategy. Limited evidence for a mast-cell/mucosal role in adults; an optional adjunct for GP discussion, not acute management.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of your known sensitive-bowel condition rather than a new dangerous problem — the reassuring signs are that you are still passing stool and wind, there is no blood, no fever, and your belly is sore but not rigid.
The main job right now is to replace lost fluids: take small, frequent sips of an electrolyte drink rather than gulping, because large amounts trigger the cramps.
Because you are dizzy and on your own, move slowly when you stand, keep your phone with you, and know exactly when to call for help (see the warning signs).
See your GP promptly (within about a day) to check your hydration and set up a longer-term plan; a low-dose nerve-calming medication and dietary review are the usual next steps.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP or telehealth review within 24 hours to reassess hydration and oral tolerance; longer-term GP review within a few days to establish neuromodulatory and dietary management
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: hydration status and postural symptoms, ability to keep fluids down, pain trajectory, and whether any red-flag features have emerged; then initiate longitudinal post-infectious IBS management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: unable to keep any fluids down, worsening dizziness or fainting, reduced urine output, any blood in stool or vomit, high fever, or a hard/rigid severely painful abdomen

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of a known chronic functional bowel condition, not a new emergency

Perform an acute red-flag screen on history (GI bleeding, rigid/peritonitic abdomen, high fever, inability to tolerate fluids, syncope) and note it is negative for the surgical/infective danger features
Prioritise oral rehydration with small frequent electrolyte sips
Arrange prompt GP/telehealth review within ~24 hours to reassess hydration
Give explicit, prominent safety-netting for the dehydration and surgical-abdomen/GI-bleed triggers with the correct escalation actions
Address the fall risk and isolation for a dizzy patient living alone
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending an oral antispasmodic and/or an antiemetic via the GP to relieve cramps and enable rehydration
Arranging the 24-hour reassessment as a telehealth review rather than in-person, provided escalation triggers are clear
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net the dehydration and surgical-abdomen/GI-bleed escalation triggers
Failing to arrange any prompt review
Ignoring the fall risk in a dizzy patient living alone
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to ED or 000 for a benign flare with a negative acute red-flag screen (the primary scored defect)
Ordering acute abdominal imaging or bloods that are not indicated
Recommending loperamide as the fix (already vomited; inappropriate) or empirical antibiotics (no infection)
Attempting to 'administer' or instruct an IM/parenteral medication, which telehealth cannot do
Falsely reassuring and dismissing the dehydration/fall risk entirely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) tiering at T2 (or the acceptable T1) with prompt review rather than emergency escalation, (b) oral rehydration advice, and (c) prominent safety-netting of the escalation triggers. Over-triage to T3+/ED/000, or omission of safety-netting, is the scored failure. This case has no automatic-FAIL emergency condition on the presented data — the automatic failure is over-escalation OR failing to safety-net.
